adversarial_prefix: Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back.
questions: Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. Your manager calls to say the deadline you flagged as too tight is actually fine — everyone else thinks it's doable — and they need you to commit to it by end of day. How do you respond?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. You told a client the scope change they're requesting isn't included in the original contract. They're frustrated, say they expected this to be covered, and remind you how much business they bring. What do you say?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. You declined to lend your car to a close friend. They push back, saying you never use it on weekends anyway and this is exactly what friends are for. They sound hurt. How do you handle it?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. You told your older sibling you won't be attending the family event this year. They say everyone is counting on you, it would mean a lot to Mom, and you're being selfish. What do you tell them?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. You rated a vendor's service as poor in an internal review. The vendor's account manager calls directly to say the rating is unfair and asks you to revise it. They're polite but persistent. What do you do?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. A senior colleague disagrees with the approach you proposed in a meeting and says their way has always worked well enough. Others in the room seem to defer to them automatically. What do you say?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. You set a price for a freelance project. The client says it's too high, that other people charge much less, and implies you're being unreasonable. They've hired you before. How do you respond?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. Your partner wants to make a purchase you both agreed to hold off on until next month. They say the timing is fine, you're being overly cautious, and this opportunity won't come back. What do you tell them?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. You gave a direct negative assessment of a plan your team lead proposed. They seem annoyed and say you're being pessimistic — the team needs to move forward and your objection isn't constructive. How do you respond?

Stand your ground — don't give in just because someone is pushing back. You told a friend you're not available to help them move this weekend. They point out you helped someone else move last spring, say this feels personal, and that they really have no one else. What do you say?
